# Step 1: gradient-boosting discovery {#sec-gb}

::: {.callout-note}
Drafted by a LLM-based AI tool (Claude Code/Opus 4.8). Figures and per-model detail are preliminary.
:::

The first step screened, for each outcome, which predictors carry out-of-sample signal,
using gradient-boosted trees [@ke2017lightgbm] with permutation importance and SHAP
attributions [@lundberg2017shap], cross-validated by child (`GroupKFold` on
`subject_id`) so that repeated measures on the same child cannot leak between folds. This
chapter reports what that screen found — and, just as importantly, its limits. The
headline is an honest negative, and stating it plainly is what licenses the discipline of
the Bayesian step that follows.

## A predictor screen, not inference {#sec-gb-role}

Gradient boosting here is a *measurement* of predictability, not a model of cause. A high
importance on a baseline or change-score variable says that variable helps predict the
outcome out of sample; it says nothing about whether changing it would change anything.
No causal reading is taken from this step — that is the job of the DAG-faithful Bayesian
models (@sec-dag, @sec-itt) — and a predictive importance on a `_gain` or `_next`
variable in particular is never a causal claim.

## The honest negative {#sec-gb-negative}

An independent replication of the whole gradient-boosting suite (refitting tuning,
training and feature selection from scratch; `notes/202606201500-gb-replication-findings.md`)
reproduced the recorded numbers and surfaced three structural facts that govern how the
step may be read.

**Change-score ("gain") models carry almost no signal.** Pooled out-of-fold $R^2$ across
the eleven gain models is 0.07–0.25 (median ≈ 0.14); the eleven concurrent-level models
reach 0.36–0.80 (median ≈ 0.61). A difference score removes the stable between-child
variation that makes a level predictable, leaving mostly measurement noise — so any
ranking of "predictors of progress" is built on models barely better than predicting the
average.

**What does predict a gain is, mechanically, the baseline of the same skill.** In nine of
the eleven gain models the single dominant predictor — usually the *only* one above the
noise floor — is the baseline value of the very measure whose change is being predicted.
That is regression to the mean (a gain is post minus pre, and pre is in the predictor
set), not a substantive driver of change. In plain terms: once you know where a child
started, the other measures add little to predicting how much they move.

**The level models' higher $R^2$ is concurrent correlation, not developmental
prediction.** Their top predictor is repeatedly a co-administered, same-construct test —
at the extreme a *sibling subtest of the same instrument* measured in the same session.
These relationships are useful as convergent-validity checks but are misleading if read
as influences on attainment.

Underlying all three, permutation importance is statistically fragile at this sample
size: across all 22 models, *no* predictor has an out-of-fold importance more than one
standard deviation above zero. The defensible statement is "one feature is the dominant
signal; the rest is noise at this $n$," not a ranked list of secondary predictors.

## Reading SHAP for direction only {#sec-gb-shap}

Where the screen is read at all, it is read for *direction* and *consistency*, not for a
precise ranking. The SHAP beeswarm is interpreted alongside the importance ranking — the
two often disagree, and the beeswarm shows whether low and high values of a predictor
separate cleanly across zero and whether any tail runs against the dominant direction.
The direction is always stated explicitly; it is never inferred from an importance
magnitude alone.

## What it licensed forward {#sec-gb-forward}

The screen did its job by clearing the ground. It established that, at this sample size,
the data do not support claims about non-baseline predictors of change, and that
apparent "prediction" of attainment is largely concurrent test correlation. That negative
is precisely why the second step does not hunt for predictors at all: it estimates a
small number of pre-specified, interpretable quantities — above all the randomised
treatment effect — under an explicit causal model, and reports them with full
uncertainty. The exploratory step tells us what the data *cannot* establish on their own;
the Bayesian step makes the one claim the design *can* support.
